name: Poster
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a recreation of Didot excess, with extreme contrast between thick and thin strokes characteristic of the Didone classification. The Monster cuts push contrast to virtuosic extremes for large-scale display use.
summary: A high-contrast Didone display family in eight styles, ranging from a base cut to an extreme Monster variant designed to push counterforms and stroke contrast to their limits.
description: Built around the excess of Didot, the family offers two optical grades: Poster for large sizes and Poster Display for even larger settings where increased contrast reads at a distance. Each grade comes in Regular and Monster cuts, the latter described as a virtuoso exercise in counterforms, with italics throughout. Eight styles in total make the family versatile across poster, editorial and titling contexts that demand high drama.
foundry_blurb: Extra Type describe Poster as a recreation of Didot excess, subtly enhanced with additional styles. The family comprises Poster and Poster Italic, a bolder Monster and Monster Italic, and four Display variants designed for even bigger sizes with more contrast and visual impact.

name: Eina
classification_notes: Eina is a sans-serif family organised into four distinct stylistic categories — rational, humanist, geometric and industrial — spanning the breadth of sans-serif subcategories, with humanist as the primary orientation given its educational and multi-purpose design intent.
summary: A 32-style sans-serif family originally commissioned as a corporate typeface for EINA University Centre of Art and Design, structured across four stylistic subfamilies — rational, humanist, geometric and industrial.
description: Designed as both a functional typeface and a teaching tool for EINA University Centre of Art and Design, the family spans four distinct stylistic categories, each available in four weights with companion italics. The design process was documented in the publication 'Four Nuances of a Typographic Standard: A Typeface for EINA', making the typeface as much a study in typographic pedagogy as in form. The four subfamilies can be purchased individually or as a complete set of 32 styles.
foundry_blurb: Extra Type describe Eina as more than a functional font, positioning it as an educational concept that goes beyond simple typographic use. Designed as a corporate typeface for EINA University Centre of Art and Design, the family comprises 32 original styles across four categories — rational, humanist, geometric and industrial — each with four weights and companion italics, documented in full in the accompanying publication 'Four Nuances of a Typographic Standard'.

name: Chamberí
classification_notes: Chamberí blends the high-contrast rationalism of 19th-century Modern Romans (Scotch typefaces) with the gestural quality of typographic Baroque, placing it firmly in the modern serif tradition with expressive display ambitions.
summary: A 56-style editorial serif designed as the bespoke typeface for Vogue España, spanning four optical sizes from functional text to flamboyant superdisplay.
description: Commissioned as a bespoke typographic identity for Vogue España, Chamberí merges the functional structure of 19th-century Scotch Romans with the gestural expressiveness of typographic Baroque. Contrast increases progressively across the four optical sizes — Text, Headline, Display and SuperDisplay — each offered in seven weights from ExtraLight to Black with matching italics. Small caps and old-style figures are included throughout, giving the family the editorial depth expected of a major magazine branding commission.
foundry_blurb: Extra Type describe Chamberí as Vogue España's bespoke typeface and an ambitious typographic branding project for one of the most iconic magazine headers in the world. They characterise it as a peculiar combination of the rational and the delicate, the sturdy and the feminine, blending the functionality of 19th-century Modern Romans with the gestural expressiveness of typographic Baroque across 56 variants modulated by contrast.

name: Blak
classification_notes: A heavy geometric sans with hard counterforms, designed exclusively in Black and Ultra weights with slanted variants, intended for large-scale bold typesetting rather than text use.
summary: A chubby geometric display typeface originally designed for the magazine Suite, available in two heavy weights with slanted companions.
description: Originally part of a type series created for the now-defunct magazine Suite, Blak pairs a strong geometric construction with hard counterforms that suit oversized headline work. The family runs to four styles across two weights, Black and Ultra, each with a slanted variant. It is built squarely for bold, confident statements at large sizes.
foundry_blurb: Extra Type describe Blak as belonging to a type series made for the defunct magazine Suite, now given a second life in their collection. They characterise it as a chubby typeface with a strong geometric feel and hard counterform, suited to big and bold statements.

name: ASM
classification_notes: ASM is explicitly described as a monospaced typeface, designed as a corporate identity font with fixed-width letterforms across all styles.
summary: Originally commissioned as the corporate typeface for Arts Santa Mònica in Barcelona, this monospaced face ran from 2008 to 2013 before becoming commercially available.
description: Designed as a bespoke identity typeface for the Arts Santa Mònica cultural centre in Barcelona, ASM is an energetic monospaced design offered in two weights with matching italics. A secondary subfamily, ASM UD, includes an underlined variant that replicated specific applications from the original corporate rollout. Eight styles in total cover Regular and Bold across both subfamilies.
foundry_blurb: Extra Type describe ASM as an energetic, monospaced and highly legible typeface, originally the custom corporate face for the Arts Santa Mònica cultural centre in Barcelona from 2008 to 2013. The family comprises two weights with italics and an underlined UD variant used in some of the original corporate applications.
